description:
# Why

bl-e454 added `bl close --resolve-remote` and the deferred-mode auto-engage.
All five tests it shipped exercise `delivery::populate_on_close` directly.
The CLI wiring — `CloseArgs.resolve_remote` -> `main` -> `cmd_close` ->
`resolve_remote || deferred` -> `close_worktree` -> `populate_on_close` —
has no end-to-end coverage.

100% line coverage does not catch this: the `resolve_remote || deferred`
expression in `cmd_close` is marked covered by the existing flag-false
close tests, so the flag-true path can regress silently — a dropped
argument, an inverted bool, a wrong default — with no test failing. Line
coverage proves the line ran, not that the flag is load-bearing.

# Scope

A `tests/` integration test driving the real `bl` binary:

- Stand up a task store plus a separate code repo containing a `[bl-xxxx]`
  squash commit; set the task's `delivered_repo` to point at it.
- Close the task from a clone that does NOT carry that commit locally,
  passing `--resolve-remote`.
- Assert the archived task (reconstructed via `bl show --json` from the
  state-branch history) carries the resolved `delivered_in`.
- Companion assertion: without `--resolve-remote`, the same close leaves
  `delivered_in` null — proves the flag is actually load-bearing.
- If cheap: a deferred-mode close with no flag also resolves, covering the
  auto-engage path.

# Note

The `delivered_repo` value written by a remote-resolved close is the
subject of bl-6816 (it is currently wrong). Keep this test's primary
assertions on `delivered_in`; if it also asserts `delivered_repo`, expect
that assertion to change when bl-6816 lands. Linked relates_to, not a hard
dep — the `delivered_in` coverage stands on its own.

# Origin

bl-e454 review pass — flagged as a test-coverage gap in the handoff notes.
title: Integration test: bl close --resolve-remote populates delivered_in end-to-end
